# About

Learn why we are building Ritual

# The state of AI is flawed.

Artificial Intelligence has the capacity to positively impact humanity but the
infrastructure in which it is being built is flawed.

Permissioned and centralized APIs, lack of privacy and computational integrity, lack of
censorship resistance — all risking the potential AI can unleash.

# Ritual is the solution.

Ritual is the network for open AI infrastructure. We build groundbreaking, new architecture
on a crowdsourced governance layer aimed to handle safety, funding, alignment, and model
evolution.

[Explore our Architecture ↗](https://ritualvisualized.com/) [Read Documentation ↗](https://docs.ritual.net/) [Meet the Team ↗](https://ritual.net/team)

# Introducing Decentralized AI.

### Censorship Resistant

Transcend geographic boundaries and closed ecosystems to participate in proliferating
the open access of models globally.

### Privacy First

Enable privacy with lightweight statistical & cryptographic schemes without heavy
performance degradation for your users.

### Fully Verifiable

Get guaranteed results from real models. Construct proofs for unbounded model sizes, for
both classical & foundation AI models, to facilitate verification.

# Built for a seamless developer experience.

Our SDK is the easiest, fastest, and most reliable way to use AI in your decentralized
apps.

```
Ritual.useInference({
	model: ["LLAMA2-30B", "Mistral-7b"],
	parameters: [...]
});
```

```
Ritual.useInference({
	model: [\
		"LLAMA2-30B",\
		"Mistral-7b"\
	],
	parameters: [...]
});
```

[Read the docs](https://docs.ritual.net/)

# Introducing Ritual Altar

### Ritual Team1 year ago

_A best-in-class program for supporting builders on Ritual is now open. [Apply here](http://altar.ritual.net/core/apply)._

## Intro

At Ritual, we have always held strong conviction that the intersection of crypto and AI will lead to both a reshaping of the existing design space of crypto applications and the creation of entirely new categories. **We also believe that Ritual Chain will be the absolute best place to build these applications, which is why we’re excited to announce the launch of [Ritual Altar](https://altar.ritual.net/), our full-stack program dedicated to supporting ambitious teams building applications at the intersection of crypto and AI from inception to beyond.**

## Building a New World

It’s no secret that infrastructure has continued to be one of the most popular categories in crypto. With a near infinite supply of new L1s/L2s, many of which offer differing promises around scalability or new execution environments or other improvements, crypto is anything but starved for blockspace.

But among this abundance of infrastructure, most that come to market are _overwhelmingly underwhelming_. Typically, this is due to a lack of novel applications, and thus, novel users. While infrastructure may unlock new narratives and new possibilities for developers, ultimately applications delivering new utility to users and retaining them is what matters.

**We know that the quality of a chain is only as good as the quality of the applications built on it.** We’ve seen this first hand. [Ritual team members](https://ritual.net/team) have:

- Worked at funds like Polychain, Paradigm, Dragonfly and a16z as investors and researchers, where we’ve helped structure rounds for core infrastructure like Eigenlayer, Celestia, Arbitrum and others, along with countless more application-layer protocols
- Built and raised millions for applications in DeFi, consumer, and more as founders and founding team members themselves
- Spent way too much [time](https://x.com/_anishagnihotri/status/1805040936113893854) (and [capital](https://x.com/akileshpotti/status/1805109817943425224)) interacting on-chain

In other words, we have built, invested in and most importantly been power users of infrastructure and applications across crypto ourselves. We understand new infrastructure promising new capabilities is only useful if applications can showcase these capabilities to users in novel product experiences. _Ritual Chain is no exception to the rule_.

On the Ritual Chain, developers will have access to a fully EVM-compatible environment while simultaneously enjoying _chain-native access to a variety of AI/ML models_. This means they’ll be able to call and use models from smart contracts, anywhere on the chain. This enables the creation of intelligent on-chain applications that inject models to enhance their feature set as well as net new protocols built around models themselves. We believe that this offers unprecedented functionality to builders on the chain, and allows for the creation of truly intelligent on-chain applications.

In addition to our expertise in building infrastructure and applications in crypto, our team has a deep background in AI and machine learning. Members have:

- Spearheaded research in applied AI/ML at Deepmind, Meta, MIT, Stanford and Berkeley
- Scaled AI/ML models at Palantir, Google, Citadel, and Jane Street

With Altar, we’ll be using our team’s full experience in crypto and AI to help builders explore and realize the incredible design space for applications at the intersection of these two categories.

## Opening the Altar

Ritual Altar is designed to be comprehensive end-to-end support for teams that want to build applications on Ritual’s infrastructure. We aim to get applications from ideation to execution to mainnet as quickly as possible. Here’s what we offer to teams:

- _Product_: Our team has significant experience building protocols and companies across crypto and AI and will work with Altar teams on the end-to-end of their product, from ideation to protocol design and development, to technical implementation. We’ll work closely with teams to incorporate and build features that make the best use of Ritual to offer entirely novel experiences and design choices to users to help their products stand out amongst the crowded crypto landscape.

- _Design_: We’ve worked with some of the biggest protocols in the space on product design, and will be bringing our expertise directly to Altar teams. We’ll help Altar teams across the full range of their design requirements, from branding & copywriting, to pixel-perfect interface design, design system libraries and UI/UX feedback and improvements.

- _Engineering_: Our team has world-class engineering experience across the stack, from frontend & smart-contract development support to building & productionizing containerized applications with composability & scalability in mind. Altar teams will have access to our engineers as they build out their product and integrate with Ritual.

- _Operations_: We have a lot of experience in helping set up companies and related operations for both onshore and offshore setups, and will help Altar teams heavily shortcut all of the “behind-the-scenes” items that go into making an enduring company.

- _Go-to-Market (GTM) and Marketing_: We will help Altar teams develop comprehensive GTM strategies that tap on our insights as operators and investors and get connected to relevant partnership opportunities. We have an extensive ecosystem of protocols and infrastructure across DeFi, consumer, infrastructure, and more building on top and with Ritual. We will help Altar teams with connecting with, integrating with and comarketing with these teams to accelerate their partnerships roadmap. We’ll also create and refine public facing content like blog posts and press releases, and engage in co-marketing to highlight them to the Ritual ecosystem and beyond to help grow their own communities.

- _Fundraising_: Members of our team have worked at some of the largest funds in the space and have led and helped structure rounds for a multitude of projects as both institutional and angel investors. Ritual Altar teams will have full access to our expertise on how to craft a story, build a pitch deck, and construct a round, as well as our network of investors in the space.

- _Hiring_: A company is only as good as its people; fortunately Ritual has access to a strong network of talent across both crypto and AI. Teams in Altar will gain access to this network, and we’ll further help you set up pipelines and efforts to hire for all your critical needs, from engineering to BD.

- _Mentorship_: We’re supported by some of the [best](https://x.com/ritualnet/status/1722381029053534611) [investors](https://ritual.net/blog/polychain) and [advisors](https://ritual.net/team) in the industry. Altar teams will have access to our network of operators, investors and researchers for feedback on their company throughout its entire life cycle.


**There is no start and end date for Ritual Altar**. Teams are welcome to apply anytime to work with us, and once we commit to start working with an Altar team, we will continue to support you as needed.

**While Ritual Altar is primarily geared to early-stage teams, we are happy to work with teams at later stages**. We welcome all builders that will build on Ritual Chain and are happy to help support them with Altar where it makes sense- regardless of where they are in their journey.

**Most importantly, we want to ensure that we are working closely with teams to build quality protocols and companies.**

## Calling for Apostles

We have been alway committed to building Ritual and its ecosystem from the ground up with our community from day 1. Ritual Altar is our next big step in doing so.

Of course, no good call to build would be complete without a request for startups. We’ve come up with a few ideas that we’d like to see built on Ritual, but we’ve taken it one step further. **We’ve gone ahead and prototyped a few of these ideas ourselves already, and would like to see teams take them all the way; [you can find them here](https://altar.ritual.net/core/rfs).** Our current list spans DeFi, on-chain consumer games, and agentic interfaces for interacting on chain, but these are only the tip of the iceberg. We’ll continue to add to this list as we come up with and prototype more ideas. More importantly, these are by no means complete. They are templates for what we believe harness Ritual’s infrastructure to deliver interesting and valuable user experiences. Beyond these, we’re always open to any and all ideas and are happy to work with dedicated teams to ideate, refine and ultimately bring them to fruition.

**Ready to become an Apostle and pioneer the next wave of crypto × AI applications? The application for Ritual Altar can be found [here](https://altar.ritual.net/core/apply)**. You can also find our team members on Twitter and DM them anytime. We look forward to seeing you at the Altar.

# Ritual × Arbitrum: Supercharging Ethereum With AI

### Ritual Team1 year ago

Today, Ritual is excited to announce that we are bringing AI capabilities to the Arbitrum ecosystem.

Arbitrum is a suite of products focused on scaling and extending Ethereum. Arbitrum One, one of the first optimistic rollups on Ethereum, has garnered over $2.4 billion in [TVL](https://app.artemisanalytics.com/project/arbitrum?tab=fundamentals) since its launch in 2021. Arbitrum has also extended its reach with Orbit, a framework that lets developers create their own L2 and L3 chains, with over [50 chains](https://x.com/arbitrum/status/1849375318752088426) already being developed. Through Ritual, developers on Arbitrum can power intelligent applications by accessing AI models directly on-chain.

## The Arbitrum Stack, Supercharged by Ritual

Blockchain developers today face significant barriers when integrating AI. Existing oracle solutions and AI setups often fail to meet the needs of on-chain AI tasks, creating gaps in both efficiency and reliability. Not only do these setups fall short of the technical requirements for robust on-chain AI, but they also add layers of operational complexity and centralization risks.

Ritual solves this problem by offering fully decentralized, holistic infrastructure that enables on-chain AI operations, eliminating these hurdles and allowing developers to access AI models directly that are enshrined on-chain.

Through this partnership, the Ritual Chain will serve as a coprocessor on all Arbitrum-adjacent chains. Applications in the Arbitrum ecosystem can access AI models directly by calling out to the Ritual Chain via a variety of General Message Passing providers.

Given Arbitrum’s vibrant DeFi ecosystem, this integration can be particularly valuable for projects seeking to boost protocol performance and accelergovernanceate governance velocity. Some early concepts we’re already exploring include [ML-enabled lending pools](https://altar.ritual.net/core/rfs/tithe-ml-enabled-lending), [dynamic fee AMM](https://altar.ritual.net/core/rfs/relic-ml-enabled-amm), and more.

## Looking Forward

Together, Ritual and Arbitrum are paving the way for a new wave of “smarter” decentralized applications. Stay tuned for further updates as the integration comes to fruition. We’re excited to see what the community builds!

For all future updates, follow [Ritual](https://x.com/ritualnet) and [Arbitrum](https://x.com/arbitrum) on X. Got ideas for Ritual and Arbitrum’s tech? Submit them to [Ritual Altar](https://altar.ritual.net/), or join our [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/ritual-net) and we’ll be in touch.

## About Arbitrum and Ritual

Ritual is building the first decentralized execution layer for AI, allowing any protocol, application, or smart contract to integrate AI models with a few lines of code. Our upcoming product, Ritual Chain, is an EVM blockchain built from the ground-up with AI/ML operations embedded natively into the chain. Ritual Chain acts as both a native environment for developers to build new kinds of crypto-applications with models at their core and as a coprocessing-layer for applications that live elsewhere. Our current product, [Infernet](https://ritualvisualized.com/#infernet), is a decentralized oracle network that allows developers on any EVM chain to access models from our node network via smart contracts. You can read more on our product page [here](https://ritualvisualized.com/).

Arbitrum is a technology suite designed to scale Ethereum. Its flagship product — Arbitrum One — is an Optimistic rollup protocol that inherits Ethereum-level security. Arbitrum One also recently updated to the Nitro stack, allowing Arbitrum’s L2 state machine (aka ArbOS) to use WebAssembly (Wasm) underneath. ArbOS is implemented in Go and included within Geth itself. This has many exciting implications including more efficient and native execution, stronger EVM compatibility, and more. Arbitrum Orbit is a new product offering that lets anyone create their own Arbitrum Rollups. Orbit Chains can be a Layer 2 (L2) chain which settles directly to Ethereum, or a Layer 3 (L3) chain which can settle to any Ethereum L2, such as Arbitrum One.

# Ritual × Arweave: Decentralized Storage For Trustless AI

### Ritual Team1 year ago

Today, Ritual is excited to announce our integration with Arweave, bringing permanent storage capabilities to the Ritual stack. By leveraging a decentralized storage solution like Arweave, Ritual can ensure that AI models, data, and proofs are stored trustlessly and are accessible to anyone in the ecosystem.

Check out the Ritual × Arweave [docs](http://ritual-arweave.docs.ritual.net/) site for more information.

## Decentralized Storage and AI Sovereignty

Decentralized storage is an essential part of the Ritual stack, integrated across both Ritual Chain and Infernet in two key ways:

Arweave will function as a storage layer for AI models—nodes running in the Ritual network can dynamically upload and download models to and from Arweave.

Arweave will also serve as a hub for artifacts that requires permanent storage: this includes model-related metadata that Ritual Chain nodes can asynchronously use for proof, privacy, or verification, as well as static content such as NFT images, product metrics, etc.

To ensure that storage requests from Ritual’s users and developers are processed reliably and efficiently, Ritual and Arweave will collaborate to establish **Dedicated Arweave Bundlers**. These bundlers streamline the workflow by aggregating multiple storage requests and relevant metadata into optimized batches before submitting them to the Arweave Network.

![Ritual × Arweave diagram](https://ritual.net/images/blog/arweave/diagram.webp)

## New AI Primitive With Decentralized Storage

AI models and the data they generate are important assets. Storing them on centralized servers not only poses security risks but also hinders the advancement of crypto-native AI applications. This integration between Ritual and Arweave offers several advantages:

- **Model Versioning and Provenance:** for models that involve sensitive computation or need to maintain audit trails, Arweave provides an immutable ledger that can store the entire history of how models are developed and updated over time. This ensures full traceability, which is crucial for proving the integrity of an AI model.
- **Verifiability and Accessibility:** zero-knowledge proofs play a crucial role in ensuring the secure and verifiable execution of AI models. Artifacts for these computations, such as ZK programs and their metadata, should be preserved transparently and tamper-proof. Ritual will store these artifacts and their metadata on Arweave, allowing users to verify computation and ensuring trust in decentralized AI systems.
- **Immutable Vector Database Storage:** operators on Ritual can leverage vector databases with data stored immutably on Arweave. This ensures that all embeddings and source data used in AI responses are permanent, verifiable, and tamper-proof - particularly benefiting RAG-based workflows on Ritual.

## Looking Forward

Our integration with Arweave unlocks new storage capabilities across models, proofs, and data. We’re excited to see what new applications and use cases our communities will come up with.

For all future updates, follow [Ritual](https://x.com/ritualnet) and [Arweave](https://x.com/ArweaveEco) on X. Got ideas for Ritual and Arweave’s tech? Submit them to [Ritual Altar](https://altar.ritual.net/), or join our [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/ritual-net) community and we’ll be in touch.

## About Ritual and Arweave

Ritual is building the first decentralized execution layer for AI, allowing any protocol, application, or smart contract to integrate AI models with a few lines of code. Our first product, [Infernet](https://ritualvisualized.com/#infernet), is a decentralized oracle network that allows developers on any EVM chain to access models from our node network via smart contracts. You can read more on our product page [here](https://ritualvisualized.com/). Our upcoming product, Ritual Chain, is an EVM blockchain built from the ground-up with AI/ML operations embedded natively into the chain. Ritual Chain integrates various co-processing sidecars that enable different compute-enhanced applications.

Arweave is a decentralized data storage network. Unlike traditional cloud services prone to centralization risks, Arweave ensures that data is immutably stored and censorship-resistant. Arweave is built to create a “permaweb” - a permanent, decentralized layer for applications and documents that ensures data immutability. This makes Arweave ideal for protocols requiring version control and historical preservation.

# Cascade: Token-Sharded Private LLM Inference

### [Rahul Thomas](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rahul-t-3b27821b3), [Louai Zahran](https://eg.linkedin.com/in/louai-zahran), [Erica Choi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/erica-yousol-choi-9932971b8), [Micah Goldblum](https://x.com/micahgoldblum), [Arka Pal](https://x.com/ArkaPal999)8 months ago

Further details on this blog post can be found in our [arXiv paper](http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.05228), as well as in our [ICML 2025 submission](https://icml.cc/virtual/2025/poster/45330).

In our [previous blog post](https://ritual.net/blog/breaking-permutations), we introduced a novel reconstruction attack that nearly perfectly reversed input tokens from hidden states of autoregressive LLMs. The success of our attack relied on the _non-collision_ of these LLM hidden states – when performing a forward pass on two different prompts, the resulting hidden states are never the same.

In practice, we saw few collisions and concluded that hidden states leak significant information about the input tokens. Building on this insight, we recognized that _reordering_ hidden state rows and elements within them did not mitigate non-collision. In other words, we could modify our attack to successfully decode certain _permutations_ of hidden states. This ultimately broke the security of three permutation-based MPC protocols which exposed permuted hidden states to the party doing inference: [PermLLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.18744), [STIP](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2312.00025), and [Centaur](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.10652).

Since even a rearranged full hidden state matrix allows recovery of the full input prompt through vocab-matching, it might seem futile to search for obfuscation protocols that do not alter hidden state element values. However, by relaxing the assumption that the _full_ hidden state matrix is revealed, we can formulate such a scheme that protects against vocab-matching: **Cascade.**

Cascade is based on the idea of _token-sharding_. The hidden state matrix at each layer has shape N×dN \\times dN×d, where NNN is the token count and ddd is the hidden dimension; revealing all of these NNN rows to an adversary, even when the NNN rows are individually permuted and then rearranged, falls prey to our attack. But what if we only reveal nnn out of these NNN token rows – do we remain secure to a similar attack? The answer is not clear-cut, and depends on the particular nnn rows rather than just the value of nnn – the **sharding scheme**. For some sparse sharding schemes, we will see that an adversary with reasonable computational power can no longer carry out our attack on the nnn rows. Therefore, Cascade aims to only reveal some of the NNN rows to different nodes throughout inference, to prevent vocab-matching.

To motivate Cascade, we will first show how to split transformer blocks into stages compatible with token-sharding.

## Transformer Inference

For transformer architectures, the layer lll transformer block can be considered a nonlinear mapping RN×d→RN×d\\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d} \\to \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}RN×d→RN×d from layer lll to layer l+1l+1l+1 hidden states, where NNN is the token count and ddd is the hidden dimension.

There is a nice way to break down single block inference into three stages. In the first stage, we perform query, key, and value projection on normalized N×dN \\times dN×d hidden states. In the second stage, we compute the output of multi-headed attention on the previously derived query, key, and value matrices, and flatten it to shape N×dattnN \\times d\_{attn}N×dattn​. Finally, in the third stage, we project the attention output to shape N×dN \\times dN×d, add it back to the layer lll hiddens, and apply the MLP, which often has a residual connection with normalization.

```
# Stage 1: Normalization and query, key, value projections.
hidden_norm = norm(hidden)		# RMSNorm, LayerNorm, or others.
query = q_proj(hidden_norm)		# Apply rotary or positional embedding here if needed.
key = k_proj(hidden_norm)
value = v_proj(hidden_norm)

# Stage 2: Attention logit computation and flattening.
attn_logits = query @ key.T + attn_mask	# Attention mask initialized previously.
attn_output = softmax(attn_logits) @ value
attn_output = flatten(attn_output)

# Stage 3: Post-projection, residual connection, and MLP.
attn_output = hidden + o_proj(attn_output)
hidden = mlp(hidden)				# MLP can have normalization and residual.
```

**Our key observation is that the majority of operations in these stages are _per-token_.** For example, in Stage 1, the query, key, and value projection maps, as well as normalization, operate _independently_ on the NNN rows of the N×dN \\times dN×d hidden states. Also, in Stage 3, the OOO-projection, residual connection, and MLP operate independently on the NNN rows of the flattened attention output (and corresponding rows in the hidden states). In fact, Stage 2 is really the only place where operations aren’t per-token.

This allows us to integrate token-sharding quite easily into Stages 1 and 3, as well as Stage 2 with a bit of additional work.

## Cascade: Token-Sharded Transformer Inference

### Pre-Pass

We first integrate token-sharding into Stage 1 to form the **pre-pass**. We would first like to split the NNN rows of the N×dN \\times dN×d hidden states h=\[h1,…,hN\]h=\[h\_1,\\ldots,h\_N\]h=\[h1​,…,hN​\] at a given layer among α\\alphaα different nodes, so that no node accesses too many rows. We call these nodes **CompNodes**, and allocate rows through **R-sharding**, which is a partition {Ri}i=1α\\{R\_i\\}\_{i=1}^{\\alpha}{Ri​}i=1α​ of rows {1,…,N}\\{1,\\ldots,N\\}{1,…,N}. This means CompNodei\_ii​ starts the transformer block with rows of hhh whose indices are in RiR\_iRi​.

Because all operations in Stage 1 are performed independently on the NNN rows, then CompNodei\_ii​ can carry out Stage 1 on its own shard of hhh, and obtain the rows of query, key, and value matrices whose indices are in RiR\_iRi​. This completes the pre-pass.

```
# Pre-pass performed by CompNode_i.
hidden_norm_Ri = norm(hidden_Ri)
query_Ri = q_proj(hidden_norm_Ri)
key_Ri = k_proj(hidden_norm_Ri)
value_Ri = v_proj(hidden_norm_Ri)
```

### Attention-Pass

At the end of the pre-pass, each CompNodei\_ii​ has query, key, and value rows indexed by RiR\_iRi​. Moving onto Stage 2, we aim to compute attention logits through query-key multiplication. However, CompNodes cannot compute all these logits in isolation! For i≠ji \\neq ji=j, there is no way for a single node to compute the dot product of a query row in RiR\_iRi​ and a key row in RjR\_jRj​, since CompNodei\_ii​ is missing the latter, CompNodej\_jj​ is missing the former, and all other CompNodes are missing both.

Not all hope is lost, though – when we arrive at a point where nodes cannot proceed further in a protocol without sharing information, a general rule of thumb is to introduce _new nodes_. To this end, we define α2\\alpha^2α2 **AttnNodes**, indexed as AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ for 1≤i,j≤α1 \\leq i,j \\leq \\alpha1≤i,j≤α. These will execute the next stage of the protocol, called the **attention-pass.**

Now, AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ acts as a sort of mediator between CompNodei\_ii​ and CompNodej\_jj​: it receives the RiR\_iRi​ query rows from the former, and the RjR\_jRj​ key and value rows from the latter, so it can compute cross-attention scores on these. In particular, it exactly carries out Stage 2, but with the query matrix replaced by the RiR\_iRi​-sharded query matrix, and key and value matrices replaced by the RjR\_jRj​-sharded key and value matrices. Finally, it stores the row-wise maximum and expsum (sums of exponentials of elements) of its computed attention logits in this process – the importance of this step will become clear in the next stage.

```
# Attention-pass performed by AttnNode_ij.
attn_logits_Ri_Rj = query_Ri @ key_Rj.T + attn_mask_Ri_Rj	# Sliced attention mask.
attn_output_Ri_Rj = softmax(attn_logits_Ri_Rj) @ value_Rj
attn_output_Ri_Rj = flatten(attn_output_Ri_Rj)

# Additional shards to store.
max_logits_Ri_Rj = rowwise_max(attn_logits_Ri_Rj)
expsum_logits_Ri_Rj = rowwise_expsum(attn_logits_Ri_Rj)
```

### Post-Pass

Arriving finally at Stage 3, we notice a subtle discrepancy: we have not actually completed Stage 2 in the attention-pass! Even though the AttnNodes computed results softmax(QKT+mask)V\\text{softmax}(QK^T+\\text{mask})Vsoftmax(QKT+mask)V with Q,K,V,maskQ,K,V,\\text{mask}Q,K,V,mask replaced by the AttnNode’s particular shards, these **cannot** simply be concatenated to obtain the true value of softmax(QKT+mask)V\\text{softmax}(QK^T+\\text{mask})Vsoftmax(QKT+mask)V. The root of this obstacle is that Stage 2 is not per-token.

Thankfully, it turns out that a simple extension of concatenation can derive the true result from AttnNodes’ partial results: linear weighting. Formally, CompNodei\_ii​ will receive partial results from all AttnNodesij\_{ij}ij​, which includes their max and expsum shards and partial attention output. Using these max and expsum shards to form a weight for each AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​, CompNodei\_ii​ can then perform a weighted average of all partial attention outputs, to get the true attention output softmax(QKT+mask)V\\text{softmax}(QK^T+\\text{mask})Vsoftmax(QKT+mask)V. This step is called **attention compilation**, and motivates our naming convention – CompNodes perform compilation, while AttnNodes focus on attention.

```
# Finish Stage 2 through linear weighting.
max_logits_Ri = elementwise_max(max_logits_Ri_R1, max_logits_Ri_R2, …)
for j in range(alpha):
	weight_Ri_Rj = exp(max_logits_Ri_Rj - max_logits_Ri) * expsum_logits_Ri_Rj
attn_output_Ri = weighted_average(
vectors = [attn_output_Ri_R1, attn_output_Ri_R2, …],
weights = [weight_Ri_R1, weight_Ri_R2, …]
)

# Rest of post-pass performed by CompNode_i.
hidden_Ri = hidden_Ri + o_proj(attn_output_Ri)	# Had hidden_Ri from pre-pass.
hidden_Ri = mlp(hidden_Ri)
```

Our final workflow for Cascade is shown below. In each layer, the α\\alphaα CompNodes begin with R-sharded hidden states, and perform the pre-pass. They send relevant query, key, and value rows to the α2\\alpha^2α2 AttnNodes, who perform the attention-pass to get partial attention outputs and related shards. Finally, AttnNodes send this information back to the CompNodes, who perform a linear weighting of partial results and then various per-token operations to get to the R-sharded hidden states of the next layer. There are other details in the diagram, like β×β\\beta \\times \\betaβ×β AttnNodes, that we will explain in later security considerations.

![High-level representation of Cascade](https://ritual.net/images/blog/cascade/diagram-01.png)

_High-level representation of Cascade._

## Do We Defend Against Our Attack?

In the beginning of the series, we mentioned our goal was an obfuscation scheme that defends against the vocab-matching attack, whilst retaining the exact results of normal inference. To make this notion well-defined, we need to explicitly adapt vocab-matching to the _token-sharded_ setting. The attack we outlined in the previous blog post assumes that the adversary has access to the full hidden state matrix, so it does not work out-of-the-box for token-sharded hidden states.

### Generalizing Our Attack to Token-Sharding

Suppose an adversary does not have access to all NNN rows of hidden states h=\[h1,…,hN\]h=\[h\_1,\\ldots,h\_N\]h=\[h1​,…,hN​\], but only kkk of them, say hi1,…,hikh\_{i\_1},\\ldots,h\_{i\_k}hi1​​,…,hik​​, with i1,…,iki\_1,\\ldots,i\_ki1​,…,ik​ in strictly increasing order.

Our modification to our original attack again utilizes the autoregressive property to reduce the search space. This proceeds iteratively, where we initialize j=0j=0j=0 and i0=0i\_0=0i0​=0.

1. Assume at this point that we have deciphered tokens 1,…,ij1,\\ldots,i\_j1,…,ij​. We iterate through all possible combinations of tokens ij+1,…,ij+1i\_{j}+1,\\ldots,i\_{j+1}ij​+1,…,ij+1​ and perform forward passes, until we find a hidden state matrix whose ij+1i\_{j+1}ij+1​-th row matches the observed hij+1h\_{i\_{j+1}}hij+1​​. This takes at most Vij+1−ijV^{i\_{j+1}-i\_j}Vij+1​−ij​ forward passes.
2. We set tokens ij+1,…,ij+1i\_{j}+1,\\ldots,i\_{j+1}ij​+1,…,ij+1​ to the ones that gave a match in Step 1. Then, we increment jjj, repeat Step 1 if j<kj<kj<k, and terminate if j=kj=kj=k.

Note that this attack only allows recovery of tokens up to and including the last hidden state index iki\_kik​, but not for tokens ik+1,…i\_{k+1},\\ldotsik+1​,….

Below is an example of this generalized attack when the adversary has access to h1,h3,h6,h8h\_1,h\_3,h\_6,h\_8h1​,h3​,h6​,h8​, meaning i1=1,i2=3,i3=6,i4=8i\_1=1,i\_2=3,i\_3=6,i\_4=8i1​=1,i2​=3,i3​=6,i4​=8. First, a search over candidate first tokens e1e\_1e1​ that matches against h1h\_1h1​ allows recovery of e1e\_1e1​ in at most V1V^1V1 forward passes. Once e1e\_1e1​ is known, a search over candidate second and third tokens e2,e3e\_2,e\_3e2​,e3​ with matching to h3h\_3h3​ can recover e2,e3e\_2,e\_3e2​,e3​ in at most V2V^2V2 forward passes. Next, with e1,e2,e3e\_1,e\_2,e\_3e1​,e2​,e3​ known, matching against h6h\_6h6​ lets us recover e4,e5,e6e\_4,e\_5,e\_6e4​,e5​,e6​. Finally, matching to h8h\_8h8​ recovers e7,e8e\_7,e\_8e7​,e8​. Recovery of e9,e10e\_9,e\_{10}e9​,e10​ is not possible with this attack — although these tokens could be inferred from the previous tokens through other means, e.g. with punctuation.
![Demonstration of generalized attack for token-sharded hidden states](https://ritual.net/images/blog/cascade/diagram-02.png)_Demonstration of generalized attack for token-sharded hidden states. Colors represent different iterations of the procedure, with the maximum forward pass cost highlighted underneath._

Since we placed no restrictions on the choice of i1,…,iki\_1,\\ldots,i\_ki1​,…,ik​, it seems at first glance that this attack works on _any_ token-sharded hidden state matrix, and can decipher the full input sequence if ik=Ni\_k=Nik​=N. This would appear to immediately render Cascade insecure, as the CompNode with the last hidden state row could obtain the full input sequence.

What’s the catch here? **The attack cost.**

In our attack, we see that at the jjjth iteration, we could require up to Vij+1−ijV^{i\_{j+1}-i\_j}Vij+1​−ij​ passes in the worst case. For a typical LLM, the vocabulary size VVV is on the order of hundreds of thousands. In Gemma-2-2B-IT, with V=256000V=256000V=256000, a gap of ij+1−ij=5i\_{j+1}-i\_j=5ij+1​−ij​=5 entails ≈1027\\approx 10^{27}≈1027 forward passes. Here, even if each forward pass took one nanosecond, the worst-case runtime would be nearly 30 billion years, more than double the current age of the universe!

**_In other words, this attack is infeasible when gaps are large enough._**

What does “large enough” mean here? This is not clear-cut, as it can depend on the use case, adversarial computational power, and many other security parameters. So, to formalize our security analysis, we introduce the **vocab-matching threshold ρ\\rhoρ**, which is the maximum value of ttt where Vt−1V^{t-1}Vt−1 forward passes can be performed by the adversary. In context of the attack, this means that once a gap ij+1−iji\_{j+1}-i\_jij+1​−ij​ is at least ρ\\rhoρ, the attack times out at the jjjth iteration. This is the key condition that ensures Cascade is secure to vocab-matching.

### (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-Sharding

Although we have generalized our attack to arbitrary choices of token (row) indices i1,…,iki\_1,\\ldots,i\_ki1​,…,ik​, it will be useful to focus on a particular sharding setup called **(c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding**. Formally, this is a form of R-sharding where each RiR\_iRi​ consists of clusters of ccc consecutive elements separated by δ\\deltaδ. For instance, below, we highlight (3,9)(3,9)(3,9), (2,6)(2,6)(2,6), and (1,4)(1,4)(1,4) sharding schemes when N=10N=10N=10. The (2,6)(2,6)(2,6) case has R1={1,2,7,8}R\_1=\\{1,2,7,8\\}R1​={1,2,7,8} in green, R2={3,4,9,10}R\_2=\\{3,4,9,10\\}R2​={3,4,9,10} in blue, and R3={5,6}R\_3=\\{5,6\\}R3​={5,6} in orange.

![Visual representation of (c,delta)-sharding](https://ritual.net/images/blog/cascade/diagram-03.png)

_Visual representation of (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding, where boxes of the same color represent a single shard RiR\_iRi​._

The motivation for this kind of sharding is that we uniformly spread gaps. This will simplify our analysis on gap sizes, although many other similar schemes could be considered.

### Large Gaps Ensure CompNode Security

We can now explain why, when only considering leakage from R-shards of hidden states to CompNodes, (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding with a large enough gap size is secure to vocab-matching.

For simplicity, consider the first CompNode, which has hidden state rows at some layer with indices 1,2,…,c,δ+1,δ+2,…,δ+c,…1,2,\\ldots,c,\\delta+1,\\delta+2,\\ldots,\\delta+c,\\ldots1,2,…,c,δ+1,δ+2,…,δ+c,…. They already know tokens 1,2,…,c1,2,\\ldots,c1,2,…,c from Layer 0 token embeddings, so the next gap in their search is δ−c+1\\delta-c+1δ−c+1, and this requires on the order of Vδ−c+1V^{\\delta-c+1}Vδ−c+1 forward passes. Recalling the vocab-matching threshold, this means δ−c+1≥ρ\\delta-c+1 \\geq \\rhoδ−c+1≥ρ is likely to make the attack infeasible.

A concrete example is shown below, for the (2,6)(2,6)(2,6)-sharding setup and the first (green) CompNode. While the CompNode can recover e1,e2e\_1,e\_2e1​,e2​ in V1V^1V1 work without issue, the next gap of size 6−2+1=56-2+1=56−2+1=5 to h7h\_7h7​ requires up to V5V^5V5 forward passes. If the vocab-matching threshold was set to ρ≤5\\rho \\leq 5ρ≤5, we would consider this infeasible.

![Visual representation of attack failure for large token gaps](https://ritual.net/images/blog/cascade/diagram-04.png)

_Visual representation of attack failure for large token gaps in (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding._

We finally note that this is not a comprehensive security analysis, since there are other important shards leaked to CompNodes (e.g. max and expsum shards, and partial attention outputs), which could potentially reveal different information than R-sharded hidden states. Still, it turns out that these additional shards do not allow vocab-matching if the gap size is again sufficiently large. For further details, see our paper.

### mmm-Splitting Improves AttnNode Security

Now that we have explained when vocab-matching is infeasible for CompNodes, we turn to AttnNodes. In our scheme, AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ receives all query rows with indices in RiR\_iRi​ and all key rows with indices in RjR\_jRj​. At first glance, assuming the shards RiR\_iRi​ were chosen to ensure CompNode security, one might expect AttnNodes to be secure. However, this is not the case: AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ gets access to query and key rows spanning indices Ri∪RjR\_i \\cup R\_jRi​∪Rj​, which has _double_ the number of indices that any CompNodei\_ii​ accesses if i≠ji \\neq ji=j!

Concretely, we consider potential security risks at Layer 000, where the query, key, and value rows are linear projections of (normalized) token embeddings, and thus could reveal the same amount of information as their corresponding embeddings. Essentially, CompNodei\_ii​ has access to tokens in RiR\_iRi​ and AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ has access to tokens in Ri∪RjR\_i \\cup R\_jRi​∪Rj​.

How do we prevent AttnNodes from getting this many tokens in the prompt? We consider an approach which makes sharding at the AttnNode level more granular than R-sharding. Formally, we alternatingly split each RiR\_iRi​ into mmm subsets, to form a new partition {Sj}j=1β\\{S\_j\\}\_{j=1}^{\\beta}{Sj​}j=1β​ of tokens {1,…,N}\\{1,\\ldots,N\\}{1,…,N} with β=mα\\beta=m \\alphaβ=mα. Now, AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ receives query rows in SiS\_iSi​ and key rows in SjS\_jSj​, so their token (row) access has been decreased by a factor of mmm. This approach is called **mmm-splitting**.

While our motivation for mmm-splitting came from reducing direct token access, the question still remains: does this prevent vocab-matching? The answer is affirmative, in the same way as our analysis of CompNodes. If mmm is large enough, there are still large enough gaps between consecutive clusters in Ri∪RjR\_i \\cup R\_jRi​∪Rj​, so as long as a gap is ≥ρ\\geq \\rho≥ρ, the attack times out.

## What Do Learning-Based Attacks Reveal?

While we have enumerated security considerations from our attack, a comprehensive analysis must consider other forms of reversal on hidden states. Most existing attacks in the literature are _learning-based_, which we break into the cases of Layer 0 (textual) and later layers.

### Layer 0

At Layer 0, CompNodes individually receive some tokens in the prompt. While we would expect little security risk from revelation of a few scattered tokens, there are serious issues when gaps between the tokens are not too large, due to the risk of _token infilling_. To test this, we use ModernBERT-large to estimate the prior distribution on input tokens and thus infill tokens. Below, we see ROUGE scores for reconstructed tokens are quite low for larger ccc (cluster size) and α\\alphaα (CompNode count), with scores below 0.10.10.1 for c,α≥8c,\\alpha \\geq 8c,α≥8 indicating nearly random reconstruction and good security.

![ROUGE-L scores for Layer 0 token infilling chart](https://ritual.net/images/blog/cascade/diagram-05.png)

_ROUGE-L scores for Layer 0 token infilling in Gemma-2-2B-IT with ModernBERT-Large tend to decrease as ccc, the cluster size, and α\\alphaα, the CompNode count, increase._

The analysis for AttnNodes is similar. Indeed, we remarked earlier that at Layer 0, AttnNodeij\_{ij}ij​ essentially has access to tokens with indices in Si∪SjS\_i \\cup S\_jSi​∪Sj​, so they essentially have the same information as a CompNode with shard Si∪SjS\_i \\cup S\_jSi​∪Sj​. Thus, for sufficiently large c,αc,\\alphac,α, we ensure AttnNodes cannot perform vocab-matching, by referencing CompNode security.

### Later Layers

To assess the capability of learning-based attacks that aim to infill at later layers, we follow the approaches of [Wan et al](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.11916) and [Morris et. al](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.13647). We fine-tune Gemma-2-2B-IT on (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-masked inputs to CompNodes at Layer 1, with a bidirectional mask to match the infilling task, and then evaluate on Layer 1 representations. As we see below, the resulting ROUGE evaluation scores decrease as ccc and α\\alphaα increase; and c=8,α≥8c=8,\\alpha\\geq 8c=8,α≥8 achieves a score near 0.20.20.2 or below, indicating little reconstruction success by the learning-based attack.

|  | **α=4\\alpha=4α=4** | **α=8\\alpha=8α=8** | **α=12\\alpha=12α=12** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| c=1c=1c=1 | 0.701 | 0.467 | 0.349 |
| c=4c=4c=4 | 0.427 | 0.290 | 0.230 |
| c=8c=8c=8 | 0.355 | 0.222 | 0.191 |

_ROUGE-L scores for Layer 1 infilling in Gemma-2-2B-IT tend to decrease as ccc, the cluster size, and α\\alphaα, the CompNode count, increase._

For AttnNodes, the use of mmm-splitting gets ROUGE scores near those of CompNodes for c=8,α≥8c=8,\\alpha\\geq 8c=8,α≥8. Following the same approach as above for c=8,α=8c=8,\\alpha=8c=8,α=8, but now using mmm-split (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-masked inputs to AttnNodes at Layer 1 for various values of mmm, we see below that m=4m=4m=4 achieves comparable reconstructibility to CompNodes.

| mmm | ROUGE-L |
| --- | --- |
| 2 | 0.3057 |
| 3 | 0.2643 |
| 4 | 0.2376 |

_ROUGE-L scores for Layer 1 infilling in Gemma-2-2B-IT, using mmm-splitting, tend to decrease as mmm increases, with m=4m=4m=4 achieving comparable performance to the CompNode score._

Thus, measures like (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding and mmm-splitting protect Cascade against existing learning-based attacks, for particular choices of those security parameters.

## Cascade vs. SMPC: Scalability vs. Security

We have shown that Cascade with a large-gap (c,δ)(c,\\delta)(c,δ)-sharding setup defends well against learning-based attacks, as well as our generalized vocab-matching attack. However, we emphasize that Cascade does not make any claims about _provable_ security, as cryptographic schemes like SMPC do. We can only offer _statistical_ security, like in our demonstrated low ROUGE-L scores of existing attacks on hidden state shards.

Given the gap in security strength between Cascade and SMPC, why should we choose Cascade? The main benefits are practical deployment and scalability, as SMPC schemes are often infeasible for larger models. For instance, one recent state-of-the-art SMPC scheme, Puma, takes around 555 minutes for a full forward pass from Llama-2-7B on a 128-token prompt. This is far too slow to be used in any real-time inference service.

We compare Cascade runtimes for various values of α\\alphaα to existing SMPC schemes MPCFormer and Puma on Bert-Base and Bert-Large:

| **Scheme** | **Bert-Base (s)** | **Bert-Large (s)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| MPCFormer | 55.32 | 141.22 |
| Puma | 33.91 | 73.72 |
| Cascadeα=1 | 0.32 \[0.31, 0.36\] | 1.01 \[0.97, 1.09\] |
| Cascadeα=4 | 0.59 \[0.51, 0.69\] | 1.57 \[1.44, 1.73\] |
| Cascadeα=8 | 0.74 \[0.62, 0.96\] | 1.58 \[1.27, 1.97\] |
| _Vanilla_ | 0.09 \[0.08, 0.12\] | 0.27 \[0.20, 0.99\] |

_Total runtime means and 95% confidence intervals in seconds over 100 trials, for a single 128-token prompt forward pass on Bert-Base and Bert-Large for MPCFormer, Puma, and various settings of newmethod. Higher α corresponds to increased node counts and security._

We run the above across machines on a WAN; for Cascade α=4\\alpha=4α=4, we use 6 machines, and for α=8\\alpha=8α=8, we use 18 machines. We see that Cascade is up to 100x faster than MPCFormer and Puma. We also note the sublinear growth in runtime as α\\alphaα increases, so that scaling to large numbers of nodes (which offers the most security) does not significantly compromise runtime.

We also measure communicated bytes for each of the methods; these are shown in the table below. Cascade requires several orders of magnitude fewer communicated bytes than existing SMPC methods, supporting operation even in poor bandwidth network conditions.

| **Scheme** | **Bert-Base (GB)** | **Bert-Large (GB)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| MPCFormer | 12.089 | 32.577 |
| Puma | 10.773 | 27.246 |
| Cascadeα=1 | 0.009 | 0.025 |
| Cascadeα=4 | 0.038 | 0.101 |
| Cascadeα=8 | 0.076 | 0.203 |

_Total gigabytes (GB) communicated for a single forward pass on Bert-Base and Bert-Large for MPCFormer, Puma, and newmethod with various settings. A prompt length of 128 is used._

Finally, we show the scalability of Cascade to larger model sizes below:

| **Model Name** | **Model Size (Parameters)** | **Mean Runtime (s)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bert-Base | 110M | 0.70 |
| Bert-Large | 335M | 1.33 |
| Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct | 1B | 2.67 |
| Llama-2-7B | 7B | 12.71 |
| Llama-2-13B | 13B | 22.72 |

_Cascade with α=2\\alpha=2α=2 and no mmm-splits scales well to larger models, Runtimes here are averages over 100 trials for a 128-token prompt._

Cascade further scales well to larger models like Llama-2-13B, and generally seems to sublinearly increase in runtime with model size. This is because for all per-token operations in transformer blocks, there is no computational overhead in Cascade’s sharding setup relative to vanilla inference. All overhead comes from attention, but it is ultimately negligible compared to the heavy matrix multiplication costs present in vanilla inference.

Thus, even as Cascade is not cryptographically secure, due to its efficiency and scalability, it holds immediate promise as a viable protocol for private large-scale LLM inference.

## Summary

Our work on Cascade stems from the following fundamental question: _How much information about input tokens do partial LLM hidden states leak?_ We motivated this inquiry from our work on the vocab-matching attack in the previous blog post, which showed full hidden states, even when shuffled, leak the full input sequence. Our results show that by choosing a sharding scheme which places sufficient gaps between tokens, the resulting sharded hidden states reveal little to no information about the input, from the perspective of both vocab-matching and existing attacks in literature. As Cascade reveals such sharded states to nodes in isolation, this provides strong statistical evidence for its security. While Cascade cannot offer the same strict privacy guarantees as SMPC schemes, it is a highly efficient protocol that offers potential for faster private LLM inference on larger hosted models.

Problem

Artificial Intelligence has the capacity to positively impact humanity but the infrastructure in which it is being built is flawed. Permissioned and centralized APIs, lack of privacy and computational integrity, lack of censorship resistance — all risking the potential AI can unleash.

Mission

Ritual is the network for open AI infrastructure. We build groundbreaking, new architecture on a crowdsourced governance layer aimed to handle safety, funding, alignment, and model evolution.

Values

Open access

AI is the most powerful technology we've ever seen and ensuring that it remains accessible to anyone is core to Ritual's mission.

Humanity-first

Ritual is building a network that ensures AI is firmly stewarded by the people—and not monolithic corporations.

Innovation over all

The intersection of crypto and AI is a rapidly developing frontier. Understanding and integrating the innovations made at this frontier is critical to the products that we build at Ritual.

Overview

# What is Ritual?

Ritual is the **most expressive blockchain in existence**, with a single focus: enrich what users can do on-chain _today_ to attract the users of _tomorrow_.

![Ritual diagram](https://mintcdn.com/ritualfoundation/a7GImEbJRnhEjeNO/assets/images/ritual-chain.svg?fit=max&auto=format&n=a7GImEbJRnhEjeNO&q=85&s=dba94c6fb09d90e3fad9e968c23be92b)

Ritual is powered by novel innovations across the blockchain stack.

## [​](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/overview/what-is-ritual\#why-does-the-world-need-another-blockchain)  Why does the world need another blockchain?

Behind every magnitude increase in blockchain users lies an innovation that
enables net-new behavior for the masses. In 2009, Bitcoin enabled anyone,
anywhere to send and receive money without relying on centralized
intermediaries. In 2015, Ethereum introduced smart contracts, enabling
developers to build permissionless, composable financial applications.Yet, somewhere along the way, we lost sight of building for the **net-new**
**user**, of expanding the status quo of what could be built with truly trustless
and expansive computation. Today’s blockchains prioritize latency and
throughput, optimizing for existing users rather than innovating. While these
performance improvements matter, they merely scale current use cases instead of
unlocking transformative new ones.**Ritual was born from a single focus: enrich what users can do on-chain _today_**
**to attract the users of _tomorrow_.**Our expedition began at the
[intersection of Crypto and Artificial Intelligence](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/overview/crypto-x-ai). Two
things are simultaneously true: it has never been easier for developers to build
with AI, crafting _dynamic_ and _personalized_ experiences, and it has never
been more important to ensure that the AI technologies we adopt remain open,
credibly neutral, and censorship resistant. To that extent, Ritual makes smart
contracts _actually smart_, allowing users to natively tap into on-chain AI,
backed by the same trustless properties of modern blockchains.However, Ritual isn’t just focused on Crypto x AI. By optimizing our underlying
architecture for any type of heterogeneous compute, Ritual hyperscales the
expressive computation that can be performed on a blockchain. Ritual is the best
venue not just for developers looking to harness
[AI, ZK, or TEEs](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/evm++-sidecars/overview) in their applications, but
also for those experimenting at the edges of the unknown, defining new ways to
consume underlying infrastructure.Our architecture [efficiently prices](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/resonance), orchestrates, and
exposes all types of compute, current or future, enabling Ritual to become the
schelling point for all of web3.Not only will end-users consume applications natively through Ritual, but other
blockchains themselves symbiotically benefit through Ritual. High-performance
chains that require a [prover network](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/beyond-crypto-x-ai/prover-networks)
(MegaETH, Movement, etc.), modern
[Rollup-as-a-Service](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/beyond-crypto-x-ai/l2-raas) offerings that require
efficient execution of heterogeneous compute (Conduit, Caldera, etc.), and the
[totality of IP](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/beyond-crypto-x-ai/model-marketplace), monetized via a
verifiable marketplace (Story, Sentient, etc.) will all use Ritual.

![Past, Present, & Future of Blockchain Execution Heterogeneity](https://mintcdn.com/ritualfoundation/a7GImEbJRnhEjeNO/assets/images/broker-timeline.webp?fit=max&auto=format&n=a7GImEbJRnhEjeNO&q=85&s=806e42099acf39beccd6ba6ef82f8559)

The complexity of what you can do on blockchain networks has grown dramatically since inception.

## [​](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/overview/what-is-ritual\#why-does-ai-need-to-be-on-chain)  Why does AI need to be on-chain?

[**Crypto × AI** \\
\\
Today’s developers have unprecedented access to AI tools for creating\\
responsive, tailored user experiences. At the same time, maintaining AI\\
systems that are transparent, unbiased, and free from undue control has become\\
more crucial than ever.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/overview/crypto-x-ai#why-does-ai-need-to-be-on-chain)

## [​](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/overview/what-is-ritual\#what-we-introduce-next-generation-blockchain-capabilities)  What we introduce: Next-Generation Blockchain Capabilities

Ritual introduces several key innovations to empower radically new on-chain
behavior:

[**Expressive, Heterogeneous Compute** \\
\\
Specialized execution for AI inference, ZK proofs, TEE code, and cross-chain\\
state access.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/evm++-sidecars/overview) [**Scheduled Transactions** \\
\\
Recurring, conditional invocation of smart contract functions, without the\\
need for external keepers.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/architecture/scheduled-transactions) [**Enshrined On-Chain AI Models** \\
\\
Train, track, and trade AI models with on-chain provenance, authenticity,\\
and IP primitives.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/ai-primitives/enshrined-ai-models) [**Model Marketplace** \\
\\
Monetize models transparently with verifiable provenance ensuring security\\
and integrity.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/beyond-crypto-x-ai/model-marketplace) [**Native Account Abstraction** \\
\\
Fundamentally expand the capabilities of EOA accounts, enabling enhanced UX.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/evm++/account-abstraction) [**EVM++ Extensions** \\
\\
Inclusion and support of in-demand EIPs that improve user and developer\\
experience.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/evm++/eip-extensions/overview) [**Resonance** \\
\\
State-of-the-art surplus-maximizing transaction fee mechanism to efficiently\\
match supply and demand.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/resonance) [**Symphony** \\
\\
New consensus protocol with dual proof sharding, attested committees, and\\
distributed verification.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/symphony) [**Node Specialization** \\
\\
Heterogeneous nodes (not one-size-fits-all) specialized for fine-tuned\\
workloads.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/node-specialization) [**Modular Computational Integrity** \\
\\
Special-purpose, opt-in primitives across provenance, privacy, and\\
computational integrity.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/ai-primitives/modular-computational-integrity) [**Modular Storage** \\
\\
Storage-agnostic approach to data, with support for web2 (HuggingFace) and\\
web3 (Arweave).](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/modular-storage) [**Guardians** \\
\\
Firewall system for nodes to granularly opt-in to execution, while still\\
participating in consensus.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/guardians) [**Native Infernet Integration** \\
\\
Ritual nodes seamlessly backed by Infernet’s compute oracle network.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/architecture/infernet-to-chain) [**Verifiable Provenance** \\
\\
Immutable records of model origin, updates, and transformations with novel\\
provenance innovations.](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/whats-new/ai-primitives/verifiable-provenance)

* * *

Ultimately, Ritual is about much more than any one use case or stakeholder
group. It’s about the collective potential of a decentralized, intelligent, and
secure computational fabric. It’s infrastructure for a world where powerful
technologies are accessible, interoperable, and trustworthy by default. And
that’s a vision worth caring about.

[Ritual in the Blockchain Landscape](https://www.ritualfoundation.org/docs/landscape/ritual-vs-other-chains)

# RITUAL FOUNDATION WEBSITE PRIVACY POLICY

Last Revised: May 2, 2025

Ritual Foundation, located at 9 Forum Lane, Camana Bay, PO Box 144, Suite 3119, George Town, Grand Cayman (“Organization”,“we”, or“us”) is the operator of any website published by Organization, including but not limited to ritualfoundation.org (the“Websites”).\[1\]

This Privacy Policy describes how we collect, use and disclose information about you when you use the Websites, as well as any of our applications, services, tools and features, or when you otherwise interact with us (collectively, the“Services”). For the purposes of this Privacy Policy, Organization is the data controller for your information, and“you”and“your”means you as the user of the Services.\[Def. 2\]

Please read this Privacy Policy carefully.By using any of the Services, you agree to the collection, use, and disclosure of your information as described in this Privacy Policy.If you do not agree to this Privacy Policy, please do not use or access the Services.

## 1.CHANGES TO THIS PRIVACY POLICY

We may modify this Privacy Policy from time to time, in which case we will update the“Last Revised”date at the top of this Privacy Policy, post the updated version on our Services and take any other steps required by applicable law. Updates to our Privacy Policy will apply only to information collected after the date of the change.Please check the Websites regularly for notices of changes to our Privacy Policy.If you do not agree to any updates to this Privacy Policy, please do not continue to use or access the Services.\[Notice\]

## 2.THE INFORMATION WE COLLECT AND USE

§2

When you use or access the Services, we collect certain information about you from different sources. In addition to the specific uses described below, we may use any of the information described in this section to provide and improve the Services and our products offerings and to maintain our business relationship,as a matter of our legitimate interests, including by enhancing the safety and security of our Services (e.g., troubleshooting, data analysis, testing, system maintenance, reporting), training the models that power our Services, providing customer support, sending service and other communications, monitoring and analyzing trends, conducting internal research and development, complying with applicable legal obligations, enforcing any applicable terms of service, and protecting the Services, our rights, and the rights of our employees, users or other individuals.

### 2.1Information You Provide to Us

§2.1

Some features of the Services may require you to directly provide certain information.You may elect not to provide this information but doing so may prevent you from using or accessing these features.Information that you directly submit through our Services includes:\[Disclaimer\]

- •
**Contact information**, such as your name and email address. We collect this information to provide Services to you as required to perform our contract with you, because it is in our legitimate interest to communicate with you (such as to respond to your interactions with us via our contact page or by email to [privacy@ritualfoundation.org](mailto:privacy@ritualfoundation.org)) or with your consent (where required, including to tell you about products or services that may be of interest).

§2.1(a)

- •
**Account information**, such as username, password, security questions that you select and the answers you provide, your account handle, profile picture and your IP address. We use this information to maintain your account and provide Services to you as required to perform our contract with you, and because it is in our legitimate interest to maintain and secure your account with us. If you choose to register an account, you are responsible for keeping your account credentials safe. We recommend you do not share your access details with anyone else. If you believe your account has been compromised, please contact us immediately.

§2.1(b)

- •
**Other information you choose to provide** in communications with us, such as your name, email address and other information you provide. We use this information because it is in our legitimate interest to be responsive to your queries, manage our relationship with you and improve our customer service and product offerings.

§2.1(c)


### 2.2Information Collected Automatically

§2.2

We, and third parties on our behalf, automatically collect certain information about your interactions with the Services and emails, including through cookies, session replay technology, and other tracking technologies (“Tracking Technologies”). Where required, we seek your consent to use these Tracking Technologies. We collect and use this information because it is in our legitimate interests to tailor your experience with the Services, run analytics, de-bug, better understand user interactions with and improve the Services and enable us to engage in more personalized advertising. We may associate this information with the device you use to access the Services, or email accounts you use to engage with us. For further information on how we use Tracking Technologies and your choices, see the section below,“Cookies and Other Tracking Technologies”.

### 2.3Information Collected From Other Sources

§2.3

We obtain information about you from outside sources, including information we receive from social media platforms, such as when you interact with us on Discord or X (formerly Twitter), which we use in our legitimate interests to communicate with you and personalize our Services. We also obtain information from the public blockchain, such as information about transactions and wallet addresses, which we use to provide Services to you as required to perform our contract with you. Any information received from outside sources that we use will be treated in accordance with this Privacy Policy. For more information, please see“Third Party Websites and Materials”below.

## 3.HOW WE DISCLOSE YOUR INFORMATION

§3

In certain circumstances, we disclose your information with third parties to perform our contract, with your consent, or as otherwise required or permitted by law. Specifically, we share your personal information, as applicable:

- •
**To vendors or other service providers who help us provide the Services**, including for cloud storage, payment processing, system administration, security, customer relationship management, management of our referral program, marketing communications, data analytics and the deployment of Tracking Technologies.

- •
**With third party payment processors, to perform a contract with you**. We will share your payment information with third party payment processors for the purpose of processing your payment transaction, if this is required for the purposes of performing our contract with you.

- •
**With our business clients, in our legitimate interests to run a successful business**. As necessary, we may share personal information to our business clients who use our services, including about your activity on their website. Transfers to subsequent third parties are covered by the service agreements with our clients.

- •
**With professional advisors, in our legitimate interests to run a successful and efficient business, or as required by law**. As necessary, we will share your personal information with professional advisors functioning as service providers such as auditors, law firms, or accounting firms.

- •
**With regulators, law enforcement agencies, public authorities, or any other relevant organizations for legal and security reasons and to protect our services and business in our legitimate interests, or as required by law**. We will share your personal information: (i) in response to a legal obligation; (ii) if we have determined that it is necessary to share your personal information to comply with applicable law or any obligations thereunder, including cooperation with law enforcement, judicial orders, and regulatory inquiries; (iii) to protect the interests of, and ensure the safety and security, of us, our users, a third party or the public; (iv) to exercise or defend legal claims; and (v) to enforce our terms and conditions, other applicable terms of service, or other agreements.

- •
**With our affiliates, in our legitimate interests to efficiently and effectively run our business**. We may share your personal information with companies within our corporate family.

- •
**With a prospective buyer, seller, new owner, or other relevant third party in connection with an asset sale or purchase, a share sale, purchase or merger, bankruptcy, or other business transaction or re-organization, in our legitimate interests to ensure the continuity of our business and achieve our commercial purposes**. We will share your personal information as necessary while negotiating or in relation to a change of corporate control such as a restructuring, merger, or sale of our assets.


## 4.COOKIES AND OTHER TRACKING TECHNOLOGY

§4

We use Tracking Technologies, including Google Analytics and Amplitude's session replay technology, to collect information about your browsing activities, including session recordings and information about your browsing over time and across different websites following your use of our Services. Such information includes:

- •
**Device Information**, such as device type, operating system, screen size, unique device identifier, Internet protocol (IP) address, preferred language and browser information.

- •
**Location information**, such as approximate location and, if you choose to provide it, precise geolocation.

- •
**Other information regarding your interaction with the Services**, such as session recordings, browser type, log data, date and time stamps, emails you have opened and clickstream data (e.g., page requests, page views, how much time is spent on a page, content viewed or interacted with, text entered, etc.).


Where required, we seek your consent to use these Tracking Technologies and will provide notice and ask for your consent before collecting any personal information that may be included in a session recording. Otherwise, many browsers accept Tracking Technologies automatically, but you are able to control the way in which your device permits the use of Tracking Technologies. If you so choose, you may block or delete certain Tracking Technologies from your browser; however, blocking or deleting Tracking Technologies may cause some of the Services, including login features and general functionality, to work incorrectly.

Your browser settings may also allow you to transmit a "Do Not Track" signal. Like many websites, our Websites are not designed to respond to this signal. To learn more about "Do Not Track" signals, you can visit http://www.allaboutdnt.com/. To opt out of tracking by Google Analytics, you can download and install a plug-in available here.

## 5.THIRD PARTY WEBSITES AND MATERIALS

§5

Our Websites may contain links to other online platforms, plug-ins or other applications operated by third parties. For example, certain features of the Services allow you to initiate interactions between the Services and third-party services or platforms, such as Discord or X (formerly Twitter) that allow you to access our pages on third-party platforms, and from there "like" or "share" our content, or to access our Services through a login integration. Use of these features may allow a third party to collect and/or use your information.

We do not control such other sites or applications, and are not responsible for their content, their privacy policies, or their use of your information. Information you provide on public or semi-public venues, including information you share on third-party social networking platforms, may also be viewable by other users of the Websites and/or users of those third-party online platforms without limitation as to its use by us or by a third party. Our inclusion of such links does not, by itself, imply any endorsement of the content on such platforms or of their owners or operators except as disclosed on the Websites. We expressly disclaim any and all liability for the actions of third parties, including but without limitation to actions relating to the use and/or disclosure of personal information by third parties. Any information submitted by you directly to these third parties is subject to that third party's privacy policy.

## 6.CHILDREN

§6

Our Websites and Services are not intended for children, and we do not knowingly collect data relating to children. Children must not use the Services, except where their parent or guardian has provided consent, if this option is available in your location. If we become aware that we have unknowingly collected information from a child, in particular children under 13 years of age, we will make commercially reasonable efforts to delete such information from our database. If you are the parent or guardian of a child who has provided us with their personal information, you may contact us using the below information to request that it be deleted.

## 7.DATA SECURITY AND RETENTION

§7

Please note that any information you send to us electronically, while using the Services or otherwise interacting with us, may not be secure while in transit. We recommend that you do not use unsecured channels to send us sensitive or confidential information. Despite our reasonable efforts, no security measures are impenetrable, and we cannot guarantee "perfect security."

We retain and use your personal information to the extent necessary to comply with our legal obligations, resolve disputes and enforce our terms of use policy, and other applicable policies and agreements. If you stop using our Services, or if you delete your account with us, we will store your information in an aggregated and anonymized format; we may use this information indefinitely without further notice to you.

## 8.WHERE WE STORE YOUR PERSONAL INFORMATION

§8

Your information may be stored, transferred to and processed outside of your country of residence, including transfers to recipients in other countries which may have data privacy or protection rules that are different from your country of residence or citizenship. For example, if we process any personal information of residents in the EEA and UK, the personal information that we collect from you will be transferred to, and stored at/processed in countries outside the EEA and UK, including in the United States, by us or one of our third party service providers or partners. We will take all steps reasonably necessary to ensure that your personal information is treated securely and in accordance with this Privacy Policy. For example, where required to do so for any transfers of personal information outside the EEA or the UK, the data transfer will be under the European Commission's model contracts for the transfer of personal information to third countries (i.e., the standard contractual clauses), or any equivalent standard clauses issued by the relevant competent authority of the UK, as relevant, unless the data transfer is to a country that has been determined by the European Commission or the relevant UK authorities, as applicable, to provide an adequate level of protection for individuals' rights and freedoms for their personal information. For more information about data transfers or to examine a copy of the relevant standard clauses, you can contact us at privacy@ritualfoundation.org.

## 9.RIGHTS

§9

Depending on where you live, you may have certain rights in relation to your personal information, such as:

- •
**Access**: The right to access personal information we hold about you, how we use it, and who we share it with.

- •
**Portability**: The right to receive a copy of the personal information we hold about you and to request that we transfer it to a third party.

- •
**Correction**: The right to correct any of your personal information we hold that is inaccurate.

- •
**Erasure**: The right to delete the personal information we hold about you.

- •
**Restriction of processing to storage only**: The right to require us to stop processing the personal information we hold about you, other than for storage purposes.

- •
**Objection**: The right to object to our processing of your personal information.

- •
**Objection to marketing**: You can object to marketing at any time by opting-out using the unsubscribe/opt-out function displayed in our communications to you.

- •
**Withdrawal of consent**: Where we rely on consent to process your personal information, you may have the right to withdraw that consent at any time by emailing us at privacy@ritualfoundation.org.

- •
**Complain**: If you think we have infringed applicable data protection laws, you may have the right to complain to your local data protection supervisory authority in the country in which you are based or where you think we have infringed data protection laws. However, we hope you will contact us at privacy@ritualfoundation.org so we can attempt to resolve any issues.


Please note that a number of these rights only apply in certain circumstances, and all of these rights may be limited by law and subject to certain exceptions. For example, where fulfilling your request would adversely affect other individuals or our trade secrets or intellectual property, where there are overriding public interests or where we are required by law to retain your personal information.

To exercise any of these rights, you can contact privacy@ritualfoundation.org.

# Community Roles
- **@Initiate** - You’re new to the community and have made it through the verification process. Welcome!
- **@Ascendant** - You have pledged to Ritual, this is the start of your community journey.
- **@bitty** - You're a little bitty baby Ritualist, on the right path, recognized, but with a long way to go. Ritty Bitty's get access to 🔥┇ritual
- **@ritty** - Long-term, loyal community member, with conviction for what we are building. You will be invited to an exclusive Telegram chat.
- **@Ritualist** - Becoming a Ritualist is the highest honor in our community. It means you have authentically demonstrated your commitment to our project.
- **@Mage** - You are a Ritualist with a mage specialization. Mages create written content and conjure art or memes that grow the community.
- **@Radiant Ritualist** - Golden Ritualist, super rare, only for real leaders.
- **@Forerunner** - Forerunners come from the time before Ritual.
